stories.title: Altitude Capital Partners Acquires Chicago’s Phantom Building for $10M
stories.excerpt: Altitude Capital Partners, a Chicago-based real estate development and investment firm, in partnership with Shanna Collective, has acquired 1447 N. Dearborn Street — historically known as The Dearborn — for $10 million. The building holds a notable chapter in Chicago’s architectural and cultural history. Originally a 50-room long-term-stay hotel constructed before 1925, it was later converted into 27 condominium units. The four-story, approximately 45,918-square-foot brick and limestone horseshoe-shaped building has been largely unoccupied since the 1990s. The acquisition was brokered by Interra Realty. Max Grossman of Interra represented Altitude Capital Partners in the purchase, while the selling party was also represented by the firm. Altitude Capital Partners plans to complete the building’s unfinished luxury condominium spaces and bring them to market. Situated in Chicago’s prestigious Gold Coast neighborhood on a 13,263-square-foot lot, 1447 N. Dearborn Street is within walking distance of Lake Michigan, Oak Street Beach, the Magnificent Mile, and dining and entertainment districts. The post Altitude Capital Partners Acquires Chicago’s Phantom Building for $10M appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Altitude Capital Partners acquires 1447 N. Dearborn, Chicago Gold Coast for $10M
27-unit condo conversion; building vacant since 1990s; 45,918 sq ft brick structure
Plans to complete unfinished luxury units; prime lakefront location near Magnificent Mile
stories.summary: Altitude Capital Partners and Shanna Collective acquired the Phantom Building (1447 N. Dearborn) in Chicago's Gold Coast for $10 million. Originally a 50-room hotel built pre-1925, it was converted to 27 condos but has sat largely vacant since the 1990s. The 45,918-square-foot, four-story brick and limestone structure sits on prime lakefront land within walking distance of Lake Michigan and the Magnificent Mile. The partnership plans to complete unfinished luxury condo units and bring them to market.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. This is a boutique condo conversion in a premium, supply-constrained submarket (Gold Coast), not rental housing. It signals continued capital interest in Chicago's most coveted neighborhoods but does not add to rental supply or inform broader demand patterns for apartments.

stories.title: Hackman’s Television City Heads Toward Sale as Studio Distress Grips Hollywood
stories.excerpt: Television City, a landmark studio in Los Angeles that has been home to productions ranging from “The Price Is Right” to “American Idol,” is poised to hit the market in another blow to owner and studio mogul Hackman Capital Partners (HCP). A lender group led by Deutsche Bank filed a notice of default last month alleging Hackman owes more than $357 million on the 25-acre property in the Fairfax District, according to the Los Angeles Times. The default follows months of negotiations between Hackman and its lenders as the company weighs alternatives, and it’s another marquee studio property in HCP’s industry-leading portfolio to fall into distress. It’s also the latest chapter in the unraveling of the industry’s bet that streaming would fuel an expansion and an extension of studio real estate demand. Culver City-based HCP over the past decade assembled the largest portfolio of studio properties, including a 2019 deal for $750 million to acquire Television City with its partner Affinius Capital (formerly Square Mile Capital) from CBS. HCP also planned a roughly $1 billion redevelopment that would add nearly 1 million square feet of offices, soundstages, production facilities and retail. But show business, and HCP’s portfolio, were significantly hamstrung by the post-pandemic slowdown, the 2023 writers and actors strikes, rising interest rates and studio consolidation. Earlier this year, Goldman Sachs took control of the 1.2 million-square-foot Radford Studio Center — where HCP also planned a $1 billion addition with another 1 million square feet of soundstage space — after the firm defaulted on a $1.1 billion mortgage. The property is now under contract to be sold to Netflix for roughly $400 million, a fraction of the nearly $1.9 billion Hackman and its partners paid in 2021. Meanwhile, Deutsche Bank has also been marketing HCP’s Manhattan Beach Studios, and has moved against other Hackman-backed assets in L.A. and New York. Television City borders Caruso’s The Grove an
bullets: Hackman Capital Partners' Television City (25-acre LA studio) enters default; $357M debt owed
Deutsche Bank-led lender filed notice of default; distress signals streaming bet failure
Industry-wide studio real estate collapse as streaming did not sustain expansion demand
stories.summary: Television City, a 25-acre landmark studio in Los Angeles' Fairfax District that produced shows including 'The Price Is Right' and 'American Idol,' has entered default. A lender group led by Deutsche Bank filed a notice of default citing $357 million in debt owed by owner Hackman Capital Partners (HCP). The distress reflects the broader unwinding of the industry bet that streaming would fuel sustained studio real estate demand and expansion. This is the latest marquee HCP property to fall into distress after a decade of aggressive portfolio assembly.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. This reflects broader capital market distress in studio/media real estate, not residential. However, it signals tightening lender risk appetite and potential reluctance to finance speculative development bets on unproven demand drivers—a cautionary signal for multifamily developers banking on untested job-growth narratives.

stories.title: Greater Boston Lab Market Edges into Positive Territory for Q2 2026
stories.excerpt: The Greater Boston lab market posted its first quarter of positive net absorption in nearly a year for the second quarter of 2026, representing what CBRE called “a tentative sign of stabilization amid persistent headwinds.” Total market availability edged up 10 basis points from Q1 and 60 bps from Q2 2025 to 32.8%, while vacancy is up 250 bps year-over-year to 28.7%. Net absorption in Q2 registered a modest positive 4,141 square feet, an improvement from the 117,186 square feet of negativeabsorption recorded in Q1. However, year-to-date absorption remains negative at 113,049 square feet. CBRE reported, “Despite the positive quarterly absorption figure, overall market conditions remain tenant-favorable. Cambridge drove the bulk of the improvement, posting nearly 194,000 square feet of positive absorption, while suburban submarkets contracted. Rents extended their decline for the fourth consecutive quarter, and vacancy rose further from the prior period, underscoring the fragility of any nascent recovery and the continued imbalance between supply and active demand.” The post Greater Boston Lab Market Edges into Positive Territory for Q2 2026 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Greater Boston lab market Q2 2026: +4,141 sq ft net absorption; first positive quarter in ~1 year
Availability 32.8% (up 10 bps Q1, 60 bps YoY); vacancy 28.7% (up 250 bps YoY)
Cambridge led recovery with 194K sq ft absorption; rents down 4 consecutive quarters
stories.summary: Greater Boston's lab market posted modest positive net absorption of 4,141 square feet in Q2 2026, the first positive quarter in nearly a year, though year-to-date absorption remains negative at 113,049 square feet. Market availability rose to 32.8% and vacancy to 28.7% (up 250 bps year-over-year). Cambridge drove recovery with 194,000 square feet of positive absorption, while suburban submarkets contracted. Rents declined for a fourth consecutive quarter, underscoring fragile stabilization and persistent supply-demand imbalance.
stories.impact: Limited direct multifamily relevance. Lab/biotech weakness in Boston does not signal residential demand tailwinds; however, tenant-favorable conditions and continued rent decline in the region's primary employment sector (life sciences) suggest wage/hiring headwinds that could dampen apartment demand growth in Boston metro over next 2–3 years despite historical strength.

stories.title: Berkadia Arranges Sale of 423-Unit MXU Trophy Property in Arlington
stories.excerpt: Berkadia completed the sale of The Commodore, a 423-unit luxury multifamily community with 18,461 square feet of ground-floor retail located in Arlington, Virginia’s Court House neighborhood.Senior Managing Director Brian Crivella, Managing Directors Yalda Ghamarian and Bill Gribbin and Associate Director Jack Canepa of Berkadia DC Metro arranged the sale on behalf of the seller, Charleston, South Carolina-based Greystar Real Estate Partners. GID acquired the property.“The Commodore represents an ideal investment opportunity in the Washington, D.C. metro area,” said Crivella. “The community combines design, a resident amenity package and a transit-oriented location within one of the nation’s strongest apartment markets.”Located at 2055 15th Street N, The Commodore is a 20-story luxury high-rise featuring 423 apartment homes above approximately 18,461 square feet of retail. The community offers a mix of studio, junior one-bedroom, one-bedroom, junior two-bedroom, two-bedroom and two-bedroom den residences, in addition to luxury penthouse homes. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com The post Berkadia Arranges Sale of 423-Unit MXU Trophy Property in Arlington appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Berkadia arranges sale of The Commodore, 423-unit luxury apt, Arlington VA for undisclosed price
GID acquires; property in Arlington Courthouse neighborhood with 18,461 sq ft retail
Greystar seller; luxury DC market asset trade confirms institutional appetite for trophy rents
stories.summary: Berkadia completed the sale of The Commodore, a 423-unit luxury multifamily community with 18,461 square feet of ground-floor retail in Arlington's Court House neighborhood, to GID. Greystar Real Estate Partners, based in Charleston, South Carolina, was the seller. Berkadia's brokerage team arranged the transaction without disclosing price.
stories.impact: Confirms active institutional bid for trophy-quality multifamily in Washington DC metro's closest-in submarkets. Arlington Courthouse assets command premium pricing and attract well-capitalized buyers (GID); signals sustained rental demand and rent resilience in DC suburbs near Metro transit. Supports underwriting for new luxury supply in densifying Northern Virginia corridors.

stories.title: Archer Aircraft Leases Entire Morgan Hill Campus from Trammell Crow Company
stories.excerpt: Air taxi manufacturer Archer Aviation Inc. has agreed to lease a 501,000-square-foot industrial complex in Morgan Hill ahead of launching its first passenger flights later this year, reported the Silicon Valley Business Journal. The San Jose-based company will lease all five buildings at Trammell Crow Company’s Cochrane Technology Center. TCC Northern California Market Leader Adam Voelker told the Business Journal the Archer lease was “a tremendous validation of both the project’s design and Morgan Hill’s growing appeal as a center for advanced manufacturing and innovation.” TCC developed the 30-acre Cochrane Technology Center, which was completed in 2024, after seeing workers commuting from Morgan Hill to manufacturing jobs farther north in the Bay Area. The Morgan Hill lease, which follows other commitments for both office and industrial space, occurs as Archer shifts from testing electric aircraft to manufacturing them at commercial scale, the Business Journal reported. The company plans to initially produce about 50 aircraft per year. Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Archer Aircraft Leases Entire Morgan Hill Campus from Trammell Crow Company appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Archer Aviation leases entire 501K sq ft Cochrane Technology Center, Morgan Hill CA
Five-building industrial complex completed 2024; Trammell Crow Company developer; air taxi mfg ramp-up
Validates Morgan Hill advanced manufacturing hub; signals job growth in Bay Area secondary submarkets
stories.summary: Air taxi manufacturer Archer Aviation has agreed to lease the entire 501,000-square-foot Cochrane Technology Center in Morgan Hill, California, developed by Trammell Crow Company and completed in 2024. The complex comprises five buildings on a 30-acre site. Archer is shifting from testing to commercial-scale manufacturing, planning to produce approximately 50 aircraft per year initially. Trammell Crow's development followed worker commute patterns from Morgan Hill to jobs farther north.
stories.impact: Significant demand signal for Morgan Hill and surrounding Santa Clara County. Large manufacturing employer (Archer) and validation of advanced manufacturing corridor accelerates job in-migration and worker relocation to Bay Area secondary markets. Opens multifamily demand opportunity south of San Jose for Archer workforce; developers should underwrite apartment supply near Morgan Hill employment nodes and transit to capture wage-earning tenants priced out of central Bay Area.

stories.title: Gantry Secures $17M to Acquire Single-Tenant Industrial Asset in OR
stories.excerpt: Gantry has secured a $16.5 million permanent loan to support the purchase of Meadowlark Industrial Center, located at 404 N. Holladay Street in Cornelius, Oregon, a key industrial submarket in Portland’s fast-growing Silicon Forest technology corridor. Recently completed in 2024, the modern property is 100% occupied by a single tenant and sits on approximately 7.35 acres. The property is a key regional location for a leading Pacific Northwest heavy equipment transporter and serves as both a traditional warehouse and for specialized uses, with abundant parking and storage. Gantry’s Paddy Ryan, Principal, Kristin Lapinskas, Senior Associate, and Hrishi Bukshin, Associate, with the firm’s Seattle and Portland production offices, represented the borrower, a private real estate investor. The 12-year loan was secured from one of Gantry’s correspondent life company lenders and features 30-year amortization. Gantry will service the loan for the lender. “Industrial property continues to be one of the favored asset classes for lender allocations in the current marketplace,” said Ryan. Hear from the West Coast’s Leading Industrial Real Estate Experts | August 20Gain firsthand insight from the executives, investors, and developers navigating today’s industrial market. From shifting capital flows and debt trends to evolving occupier demand and development strategies, take part in the conversations shaping the future of industrial real estate. Register now: www.connectindustrialwest2026.com The post Gantry Secures $17M to Acquire Single-Tenant Industrial Asset in OR appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Gantry secures $16.5M permanent loan for Meadowlark Industrial, Cornelius OR (Portland Silicon Forest)
100% occupied by single heavy equipment transporter tenant; recently completed 2024; 7.35 acres
12-year loan, 30-year amortization from life company; validates industrial asset strength in Portland corridor
stories.summary: Gantry arranged a $16.5 million permanent loan from a correspondent life company to support acquisition of Meadowlark Industrial Center in Cornelius, Oregon, a key Portland Silicon Forest submarket. The recently completed (2024) property is 100% occupied by a single tenant (Pacific Northwest heavy equipment transporter) and sits on approximately 7.35 acres. The 12-year loan features 30-year amortization and will be serviced by Gantry.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. Industrial financing strength (especially from life companies) reflects investor confidence in Portland area logistics and manufacturing demand. However, this signals healthy capital deployment in adjacent asset classes and regional economic vitality; developers should note Silicon Forest job base and employer growth support multifamily pipeline in and around Cornelius and suburban Portland markets.

stories.title: Judge tosses suit over Two Roads’ Four Seasons Bahamas. Not so fast, plaintiff says
stories.excerpt: A judge tossed a lawsuit accusing Two Roads Development of sidelining a partner from a roughly $300 million Four Seasons-branded condo project in the Bahamas –– but the fight isn’t over.  The partner that sued, Soho Development, led by Delray Beach resident and attorney Roger Stein, says the judge addressed the wrong complaint and not the latest filing. Soho Development last summer sued Two Roads, a South Florida luxury development firm, alleging it excluded Soho from the development, while continuing to use project contracts and confidential information provided by Soho with no compensation to the firm, according to the complaint […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Two Roads Development and Access Industries (Len Blavatnik) develop 67-unit Four Seasons condo, Bahamas
Ocean Club luxury project on 6.2-acre Paradise Island site; presales underway; completion 2028
Soho Development litigation over alleged exclusion and IP misuse; non-compete dispute; deal value ~$300M
stories.summary: Two Roads Development and Access Real Estate (backed by billionaire Len Blavatnik) are developing Ocean Club, Four Seasons Residences Bahamas, a 67-unit luxury condo project on 6.2 acres of waterfront on Paradise Island. The project features two- to five-bedroom units and villas with presales underway and completion expected in 2028. A separate lawsuit filed by partner Soho Development alleges Two Roads excluded Soho from the project while using confidential information; a judge initially tossed the suit, though Soho contests the ruling.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. This is luxury condo development in a Caribbean resort destination, not apartment rental housing in major U.S. metro job centers. Litigation signals partner/IP disputes typical of large development deals but does not inform domestic apartment market fundamentals or capital availability.

stories.title: Mamdani Selects Underutilized NYPD Parking Lot for Affordable Housing
stories.excerpt: Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced Monday that an underutilized New York Police Department parking lot in Manhattan’s East Village will be redeveloped as mixed-use housing. The site, at 324 East Fifth Street, is slated for approximately 131 affordable housing units, 30 percent of which will be reserved for formerly homeless New Yorkers. The mixed-use project was designated to a development team of Spatial Equity, Housing Works, the Cooper Square Committee and the This Land Is Ours Community Land Trust. The mayor announced the redevelopment plans jointly with Deputy Mayor Leila Bozorg and Dina Levy, head of New York City Housing Preservation and Development. The development, dubbed the Aurea, will include a senior center, community space and replacement parking. Plans also include landscaped terraces, green roofs and all-electric systems. Housing Works will provide on-site support services for residents. On his first day in office this year, Mayor Mamdani mandated a review of city-owned sites that could be used to develop at least 25,000 new housing units over the next decade. The Land Inventory Fast Track (LIFT) task force was created for the purpose. “This project will provide permanently affordable housing, create homes for formerly homeless New Yorkers and put community stewardship at its center through a community land trust,” Mamdani said in a statement. “It’s the first city land designation of our administration, and it’s exactly the kind of housing we’re committed to building across the five boroughs: deeply affordable, community-led and worthy of the greatest city in the world.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Mamdani administration converts NYPD parking lot, 324 East 5th St, into 131-unit affordable housing
East Village site; 30% units reserved for formerly homeless; mixed-use with senior center, replacement parking
Spatial Equity, Housing Works, Cooper Square Committee, This Land Is Ours partnership; all-electric green systems
stories.summary: New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani announced redevelopment of an underutilized NYPD parking lot at 324 East Fifth Street in Manhattan's East Village as the Aurea, a mixed-use project with approximately 131 affordable housing units. Thirty percent of units will be reserved for formerly homeless New Yorkers. The development team comprises Spatial Equity, Housing Works, Cooper Square Committee, and This Land Is Ours Community Land Trust. The project will include a senior center, community space, replacement parking, landscaped terraces, green roofs, and all-electric systems.
stories.impact: Signals NYC administration commitment to conversion of publicly held underutilized sites for below-market rental housing. While this specific project targets deep affordability, it demonstrates regulatory appetite for housing-first site assembly and expedited entitlement on public land in tight East Village submarket. For-profit developers should monitor public site releases and potential fast-track review processes; Mamdani's mandate to review city-owned sites signals steady pipeline of conversion opportunities.

stories.title: From the gridiron to the high-rise: Blake Poston planning $100M Uptown Dallas high-rise
stories.excerpt: A former SMU football player is developing a $100 million, 20-story high-rise just north of Uptown Dallas, around a mile from where he used to drop into coverage on Saturdays.  Blake Poston, who was a part of the Southern Methodist University Mustangs football team for three years and went to Highland Park High School, is also the founder of Cover2 Capital. Poston told the Dallas Morning News that Cover2 is aiming to begin construction on an Uptown high-rise at 5220 McKinney Avenue late next year. The building is dubbed The Savannah, and Cover2 is still in design and city development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Cover2 Capital (Blake Poston, SMU alum) plans $100M, 20-story, 100-unit luxury apt, Uptown Dallas
The Savannah at 5220 McKinney Ave; 1,800 sq ft avg units; boutique positioning; construction late 2025
Developer also behind 297-unit Wheelhouse (North Richland Hills) and Greerhouse garden-style project (Anna)
stories.summary: Blake Poston, founder of Cover2 Capital and former SMU football player, is developing The Savannah, a $100 million, 20-story luxury apartment building in Uptown Dallas near McKinney Avenue with approximately 100 units, including three penthouse apartments. The boutique community will feature one- to three-bedroom units averaging 1,800 square feet. Rents will be priced similarly to nearby luxury properties. Cover2 is currently in design and city review processes with construction planned to begin late 2025. Poston's firm also developed the 297-unit Wheelhouse in North Richland Hills and is planning the Greerhouse garden-style project in Anna.
stories.impact: Signals sustained luxury renter demand and developer confidence in Uptown Dallas' density and appeal. The Savannah's large unit sizes (1,800 sq ft avg) and penthouse positioning target high-income renters and suggest limited new luxury supply in that submarket. Strong execution track record (Wheelhouse) indicates execution capability; developers should monitor Uptown infill for potential gap in mid-to-large luxury units and consider acquisition/development windows before Cover2 matures product line.

stories.title: Real Estate Firm Landmark Management Signs 12K-SF Lease at One Grand Central Place
stories.excerpt: A real estate investment advisor is relocating its offices to Midtown East, near Grand Central Terminal, Commercial Observer has learned. Landmark Management signed a 12,168-square-foot lease on the 52nd floor of Empire State Realty Trust (ESRT)’s One Grand Central Place, according to the landlord, in a relocation from its current offices at 115 East 69th Street on the Upper East Side. ESRT did not disclose the asking rent or the length of the lease, but the average office asking rent in Midtown during the second quarter of 2026 was $86.18 per square foot, according to a report from CBRE. Ryan Kass, Jordan Berger, Shanae Ursini and Kerry Lavelle represented ESRT in-house alongside a Newmark team of Scott Klau, William Cohen, Erik Harris, Neil Rubin, Zachary Weil and Cole Gendels. CBRE’s Christopher Corrinet and Evan Fiddle negotiated on behalf of the tenant. Newmark and CBRE did not immediately respond to requests for comment. Other tenants in the 55-story building along East 42nd between Madison and Park avenues Street include financial and consulting services firm Gerson Lehrman Group, which expanded to 95,612 square feet there in November 2025, as well as fintech firm iCapital, which expanded to 219,928 square feet in February 2025. J.P. Morgan Chase Bank also operates a large retail location at the base of the building. Chase renewed its 21,683-square-foot lease in February 2026. Mark Hallum can be reached at mhallum@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Landmark Management leases 12,168 sq ft on 52nd floor, ESRT's One Grand Central Place, NYC
Midtown East relocation from Upper East Side; mid-market office; ~$86/sq ft avg asking rent Q2 2026
Broker lineup: ESRT in-house + Newmark (landlord rep), CBRE (tenant rep); institutional office market traction
stories.summary: Real estate investment advisor Landmark Management signed a 12,168-square-foot lease on the 52nd floor of Empire State Realty Trust's One Grand Central Place in Midtown East, relocating from Upper East Side offices at 115 East 69th Street. The deal terms (rent, lease length) were not disclosed, but average office asking rent in Midtown during Q2 2026 was $86.18 per square foot. Empire State Realty Trust and brokerage teams from Newmark and CBRE represented the landlord and tenant, respectively.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily impact. This is office leasing activity in Manhattan and does not signal residential demand. However, office market activity (even modest tenancy) in Midtown indicates some recovery in institutional office leasing post-pandemic; sustained Manhattan office rents ($86/sq ft) suggest viable job base and worker presence that underpins residential demand in surrounding neighborhoods.

stories.title: Kolter Expands Miami Beach Assemblage for Condo Dev With BH Group
stories.excerpt: Kolter Group and BH Group have expanded their oceanfront assemblage in Miami Beach’s North Beach neighborhood, where they plan to build a 17-story luxury condo development. In one transaction, Kolter Group bought 10 condo units within the Port Royale Condo at 6969 Collins Avenue for about $3.8 million, property records show. The 14-story structure, built in 1971, has faced concerns over its structural integrity, and in 2022 residents of the 172-unit building were evacuated. In another deal on the same block, Kolter paid $14.4 million for the 7,600-square-foot commercial condos of the Crystal Beach Suites Hotel at 6981 Collins Avenue. The sellers were Miami Beach-based Elysee Investment Company, which owned a 70 percent stake, and Joseph Cohen, who owned the rest. The purchase likely brings the 1950-era, 1-acre property under the full control of the developers. In 2023, the Delray Beach-based developer purchased the 84-room hotel component for $18 million. In January, Kolter bought the four-story, 0.3-acre property sandwiched between the Port Royale Condo and Crystal Beach Suites Hotel, paying $26 million. Wells Fargo provided a $41 million mortgage. Put together, the acquisitions give the developers ownership stakes in roughly half of the oceanfront properties between 69th and 71st streets. On at least a portion of the block, the developers plan to replace the aging structures with a 37-unit condo development designed by Kobi Karp. The project is set to include 947 square feet of ground-floor retail and 86 parking spots. Last year, the joint venture marketed a 1-acre section of the site for $110 million. A representative for the joint venture did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Julia Echikson can be reached at jechikson@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Kolter Group and BH Group expand Miami Beach North Beach assemblage for 17-story luxury condo
Kolter buys 10 units Port Royale Condo ($3.8M) and Crystal Beach commercial condo ($14.4M)
Port Royale (1971, 172 units) structural concerns; 2022 evacuation; assemblage strategy targeting distress
stories.summary: Kolter Group and BH Group have expanded their oceanfront assemblage in Miami Beach's North Beach neighborhood for a planned 17-story luxury condo development. Kolter purchased 10 units within the 14-story Port Royale Condo (built 1971, 172 units) for approximately $3.8 million and bought the 7,600-square-foot commercial component of the adjacent Crystal Beach Suites Hotel for $14.4 million. The Port Royale has faced structural integrity concerns and residents were evacuated in 2022. The assemblage likely consolidates developer control for comprehensive redevelopment.
stories.impact: Signals continued trophy condo development appetite in Miami Beach and willingness to acquire distressed/aging assets for demolition and replacement with high-end residential. North Beach oceanfront premium pricing validates rental and condo market strength; assembly strategy targets aging 1970s stock, suggesting pipeline opportunities for multifamily conversion or new construction targeting similar locations with older building stock and out-of-pocket maintenance obligations.

stories.title: Apollo Provides $71M Loan for Tribeca Luxury Condo Conversion
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon has secured $71 million in construction financing to build 139 Franklin Street, a boutique residential condominium that will deliver up to 18 units in Manhattan’s Tribeca neighborhood, Commercial Observer can first report. Affiliates of Apollo Global Management provided the construction debt, while a JLL Capital Markets team of Michael Gigliotti and Aaron Niedermayer arranged the transaction. Raymond Chalmé, principal of Broad Street Development, described the new asset as “one of Tribeca’s most distinctive boutique residential offerings,” in a statement and noted that the building is being marketed specifically to luxury buyers in the swanky Manhattan submarket. “We appreciate Apollo’s confidence in our team and JLL’s expertise in arranging financing that positions the development for a successful delivery,” he said. Located near the intersection of Franklin Street, Varick Street and West Broadway, 139 Franklin Street is being built on the site of a 56,384-square-foot, 10-story, 74-unit self-storage facility. The site is in the heart of Tribeca, an enclave of lofts and converted luxury apartments on the west side of Manhattan below Canal Street. Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon acquired the former site from the Sofia family for $43.5 million in 2025, according to YIMBY. This is far from Broad Street’s first luxury or conversion play in Lower Manhattan. The firm oversaw the construction of 40 Bleecker Street and 215 Sullivan Street in the last decade, and converted the old Maritime Exchange Building into 326 luxury apartments at 80 Broad Street. The new residential condo at 139 Franklin Street is expected to deliver three- to five-bedroom homes with private outdoor space and wellness-style amenities, according to JLL. JLL’s Gigliotti described Tribeca in a statement as one of Manhattan’s most sought-after residential submarket due to the limited invento
bullets: Apollo Global Management provides $71M construction loan for Broad Street Dev / TPG Angelo Gordon
139 Franklin Street, Tribeca; 18-unit boutique luxury condo conversion; JLL Capital Markets arranger
Tribeca luxury condo financing strength; 16K+ sq ft lot; near Franklin/Varick/West Broadway intersection
stories.summary: A joint venture between Broad Street Development and TPG Angelo Gordon secured $71 million in construction financing from Apollo Global Management affiliates for 139 Franklin Street, a boutique luxury residential condominium in Manhattan's Tribeca. The project will deliver up to 18 units and is being marketed to luxury buyers. JLL Capital Markets arranged the transaction. The property is located near the intersection of Franklin, Varick, and West Broadway.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily impact. This is luxury condo conversion (18 units) targeting high-net-worth buyers, not rental housing. However, the deal demonstrates Apollo's continued allocation to Manhattan residential development and stable construction financing availability for trophy projects in desirable submarkets—a positive signal for multifamily construction lending appetite in comparable-quality assets in NYC metro.

stories.title: South Florida Dirt: A timeline of the Vacchi vs. Stern legal battle
stories.excerpt: Developer Michael Stern teamed up with Italian investor and TikToker Gianluca Vacchi in 2024.  At the time, Stern said that Vacchi “understands new media and how to cut through the noise on social media in a way most real estate people don’t,” calling him an effective marketer and tastemaker. Online to his 22.1 million Instagram followers and 22.3 million TikTok followers, Vacchi has showcased his jetsetting lifestyle, workouts, cold plunges, and maybe most frequently, his dancing.  Two years ago, Stern and Vacchi said they were 50-50 partners on a $4 billion pipeline of JDS Development’s projects (Stern’s firm). That included […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Michael Stern (JDS Development) and Gianluca Vacchi (22M Instagram followers) partnership implodes
Vacchi sues alleging Ponzi scheme; disputes $4B pipeline including Mercedes-Benz Places, Dolce & Gabbana 888 Brickell
Mercedes-Benz and D&G projects in litigation; JDS seeking new debt and partner (Jeff Soffer) to rescue
stories.summary: Developer Michael Stern (JDS Development) and Italian investor/social media personality Gianluca Vacchi (22.1M Instagram, 22.3M TikTok followers) have had their partnership collapse into litigation. Vacchi sued Stern alleging he ran a Ponzi scheme and misrepresented project status while diverting investment capital across developments. The dispute involves a $4 billion development pipeline including Mercedes-Benz Places Miami, the Dolce & Gabbana-branded 888 Brickell, and the 1250 West Avenue project in Miami Beach. JDS has exited 1250 West Avenue and is seeking new debt and partnerships (including Jeff Soffer) to rescue the stalled Mercedes-Benz and D&G projects.
stories.impact: Signals distress in South Florida luxury condo development and potential capital constraints at mid-sized developer (JDS). Public litigation and project stalls reduce forward-looking condo-to-rent conversion opportunities and may indicate investor reticence on signature Miami Beach projects. For multifamily developers, this underscores execution risk in co-development partnerships with celebrity/influencer partners and suggests opportunities to acquire distressed or stalled sites at discount if JDS or JDS partners require capital infusion or exit.

stories.title: Mark Cuban takes Adelson to court on Mavs plans for new arena
stories.excerpt: Who could’ve guessed that selling a basketball team would change who’s in charge? Apparently not Mark Cuban.  After hinting at his regret over selling the Dallas Mavericks to Las Vegas Sands on a podcast in March, the billionaire businessman isn’t just going public with his concerns about the team’s new ownership–he’s taking them to court.  Cuban, who sold his majority stake in the team to Miriam Adelson’s casino company in 2023, claims in a new court filing that he was shut out of decisions about the team’s new arena, the Dallas Morning News reported. He’s asking the court to grant […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Mark Cuban sues Las Vegas Sands/Miriam Adelson over Dallas Mavericks arena decision-making; Valley View Mall site
Cuban sold majority stake 2023; claims Sands excluded him despite verbal ops agreement; wants contract review
Arena contingent on Texas casino legalization; decade-long lobbying effort; Dončić trade escalated rift
stories.summary: Mark Cuban, former majority owner of the Dallas Mavericks, filed a lawsuit against Las Vegas Sands and owner Miriam Adelson claiming he was shut out of decisions regarding the team's planned new arena at the Valley View Mall site. Cuban, who sold his majority stake to Sands in 2023, alleges a verbal agreement entitled him to continue overseeing team operations while Sands pursued development. The arena project is contingent on Texas legalizing casino gambling, a long-standing legislative goal for both parties. Cuban cited the Dončić trade—which he learned about too late—as a breaking point in the relationship.
stories.impact: No direct multifamily impact. This is sports franchise/gaming development litigation unrelated to apartment markets. However, the blocked Dallas arena deal signals that Texas casino legalization remains uncertain despite years of lobbying, which reduces near-term potential for large mixed-use hospitality-residential developments tied to gaming hubs in Texas markets (Dallas, Austin, San Antonio).

stories.title: Nonprofit pitches housing conversion at Palo Alto site with SB 79 proposal
stories.excerpt: A small office building in Palo Alto could give way to a multifamily building not far from one of the city’s Caltrain stops.  San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposed the plan to erect a six-story building at 555 College Avenue with 70 apartments and roughly 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, Mercury News reported. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million, according to documents filed with the Santa Clara County Recorder’s Office.  The building would consist of five stories of housing above a ground floor that could accommodate retail, dining or other commercial uses. The […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Minority Television Project (nonprofit) proposes 70-unit, six-story apt via SB 79, Palo Alto 555 College Ave
Purchased property 2023 for $7.1M; near California Avenue Caltrain; 8K sq ft ground-floor commercial
Also proposing 24-unit office-to-residential conversion at 127 Lytton Ave using SB 79 transit-oriented provisions
stories.summary: San Mateo-based nonprofit Minority Television Project proposed a six-story, 70-unit apartment building at 555 College Avenue in Palo Alto with approximately 8,000 square feet of ground-floor commercial space, using provisions of Senate Bill 79 (effective Jan. 2025). The law allows taller housing within a half-mile of qualifying transit hubs; in this case, the California Avenue Caltrain station. The nonprofit purchased the property in 2023 for $7.1 million. The organization has also proposed a separate 24-unit office-to-residential conversion at 127 Lytton Avenue, also using SB 79 approvals.
stories.impact: SB 79 represents material regulatory tailwind for Bay Area transit-oriented multifamily development. Palo Alto faces a 6,000-unit entitlement goal over five years; Minority Television Project's dual filings signal nonprofit and for-profit developer appetite to unlock office-to-residential conversions and densification near transit. For multifamily developers, this validates SB 79 feasibility and suggests immediate opportunity to acquire or partner on office assets within half-mile of Caltrain/BART stations in Palo Alto, Mountain View, and San Jose before land/entitlement scarcity pushes pricing.

stories.title: Steel IQ’s 3D-printed steel could slash builds from months to weeks
stories.excerpt: The new Lake Dallas startup Steel IQ believes that its 3D digital extrusion and printing is the sustainable future for affordable housing in America.  One of the major problems in the United States’ development pipeline is a lack of skilled construction workers. Steel IQ’s 3D printed steel frames may cost more to install, but require fewer skilled workers and cut down on framing timelines. The process uses a  digital model of a building, finds its dimensions with AI assistance and prints it in steel, down to the holes for the screws, according to the Dallas Morning News. Steel IQ’s founder […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Steel IQ (Lake Dallas startup) offers 3D-printed steel framing; cuts construction timeline and labor needs
Frames printed with AI-assisted digital models; assembled on-site; zero trees; resistant to weather
Cost premium offset by waste reduction, speed, fewer skilled workers; used in luxury home (Infinity mansion, Dallas)
stories.summary: Lake Dallas startup Steel IQ has developed a 3D digital extrusion and printing system for steel building frames designed to address the U.S. construction labor shortage. The process uses a digital model and AI assistance to print structural frames with pre-drilled holes, enabling assembly on-site like modular components. Steel IQ founder Nejeeb Khan says the system reduces waste, construction time, and labor requirements despite higher material cost than wood framing. The technology uses zero trees and is more weather-resistant. The firm's framing was recently used in the $15 million 'Infinity' mansion in frisco, Dallas, listed at $1,106 per square foot.
stories.impact: Emerging construction technology with potential to improve affordable housing economics if cost premium compresses. Steel IQ's labor-reduction and timeline benefits directly address skilled trades shortage plaguing multifamily development, particularly in secondary and tertiary markets. Developers should pilot-test on future projects; if cost-per-unit competitive with wood framing post-scale, this could unlock faster lease-up on cost-sensitive value-add and workforce housing in labor-constrained markets (Texas, Southeast).
